title: The HTML DOM 🌐
description: Understand how JavaScript actually connects to the browser window.

slides.title: Escaping the Console 🏃‍♂️
slides.content:
# The Real World

Until now, every piece of code we've written has simply done math and printed white text to a hidden developer terminal: `console.log()`.

But that's not what the user sees!

Users look at HTML Webpages. If we want JavaScript to be useful, it needs to step out of the shadows, grab an HTML file, and physically manipulate the web page!

slides.title: The Tree of Life 🌳
slides.content:
## What is the DOM?

When Google Chrome (or Safari) downloads an HTML webpage, it physically takes all of those `<tag>` brackets and converts them into a massive, organized tree of JavaScript Objects.

This tree is called the **Document Object Model (DOM)**.

The DOM is the absolute ultimate bridge between static HTML and lightning-fast JavaScript.

slides.title: The Window Object 🪟
The Document Object 📄
slides.content:
## The Chosen One

Inside that massive `window` tab, there is one very special object: **`document`**.

The `document` object represents the actual HTML Website currently loaded on the screen.

```javascript
// Change the background color of the entire website!
document.body.style.backgroundColor = "red";
```

JavaScript can literally reach out, grab the website's body, and manipulate the CSS instantly. No page reloading required!

quizData.question: What does DOM stand for?
quizData.explanation: The 'Document Object Model' is the tree-like structure the browser builds specifically so JavaScript can easily grab and modify the HTML tags.

quizData.question: Which Global Object strictly represents the loaded HTML Webpage sitting inside the browser tab?
quizData.explanation: While 'window' handles the entire browser tab mechanics (like scrolling), the 'document' object specifically holds the entire HTML DOM. You always start with document.__
